prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One commonly-praised practice in modern knowledge work that I believe is overrated is the emphasis on constant, real-time communication and collaboration through tools like Slack or Microsoft Teams. While these tools can certainly be useful for quick questions or urgent matters, the expectation of being always available and responsive can lead to frequent interruptions and context switching that ultimately undermine deep, focused work.

The ability to engage in sustained, uninterrupted concentration is critical for tackling complex problems, generating creative insights, or producing high-quality outputs. But when knowledge workers feel pressured to constantly monitor and respond to incoming messages and notifications, their attention becomes fragmented. It becomes difficult to sink into the kind of flow state where meaningful progress happens.

Additionally, an overreliance on real-time communication can sometimes come at the expense of more thoughtful, asynchronous forms of collaboration. Carefully crafted documents, memos, or emails that lay out ideas in detail may be undervalued compared to the quick back-and-forth of instant messaging. But these more deliberate forms of communication often lead to clearer thinking, better documentation of institutional knowledge, and more inclusive participation from team members who may not always be able to engage in real-time discussions.

So while real-time collaboration tools have their place, I believe organizations would do well to establish clearer norms and boundaries around their use, and to place greater value on protecting stretches of focused work time and leveraging more asynchronous modes of communication where appropriate. The goal should be enabling both productive collaboration and deep individual work, rather than allowing the former to constantly interrupt the latter.
